titleStmt.title: On the depth of cylindrical indentation of an elastic half-space for two types of displacement constraints

affiliation.orgName: Department of Mechanical and Aerospace Engineering

analytic.title: On the depth of cylindrical indentation of an elastic half-space for two types of displacement constraints

application.desc: GROBID - A machine learning software for extracting information from scholarly documents

div.p: For cylindrical indentation of elastic half-space the relationship between the depth of indentation δ and the applied force F is nonlinear, in contrast to the linear relationship between the height of the contact zone δ0 and the force F . While the latter is independent of the boundary conditions used to specify the rigid-body translation, the former depends on a selected datum for vertical displacement. The depth of the indentation is determined for any permissible value of the length b, which specifies the points of the free surface where the vertical displacement is required to be zero, w(±b) = 0. From the condition that the work of the indentation force is equal to the work of the contact pressure, it follows that the indentation is geometrically and physically possible under imposed boundary conditions w(±b) = 0 provided that b ≥ bmin. The numerical value of bmin is found to be about 10 times greater than the semi-width of the contact zone a, based on the numerical precision in fulfilling the work condition WF = Wp. If a datum is taken to be at a point at some distance h below the load, there is an alternative closed-form expression for δ in terms of F , which involves the Poisson ratio. For ν = 1/3, it is found that hmin is about 21a. A simple expression relating the permissible values of h and b is derived, which is linear for large values of h and b.

div.head: Introduction
div.p: The depth δ of spherical and conical indentations of an elastic half-space is uniquely related to the indentation force F , because a datum for vertical displacement can be taken to be at infinity, where the displacement goes to zero with the distance r from the load as 1/r. For spherical indentation F = (4 √ R/3)E * δ 3/2 , where E * = E/(1 -ν 2 ) is the effective modulus of elasticity, expressed in terms of Young's modulus of elasticity E and Poisson's ratio ν, and R is the radius of a spherical indenter. The corresponding height of the contact zone is δ 0 = δ/2. For conical indentation, the quadratic relationship F = (2 tan α/π)E * δ 2 holds, where α is the cone angle, while the corresponding height of the contact zone is δ 0 = 2δ/π. In contrast to these well-known results [1]- [4], the relationship between F and δ for cylindrical indentation of a half-space has not been elaborated upon in the literature, probably because it was recognized that the displacements logarithmically increase with the distance from the load, preventing a datum to be taken at infinity, which means that δ depends on a selected point at which the vertical displacement is required to be zero. Most commonly, this datum is taken to be at some vertical distance h below the load, or through a pair of points on the free surface at some horizontal distance b from the center of the load. For finite-size bodies, the depth of indentation also depends on the size and shape of indented body and the location of the indenter relative to the boundaries of the body [2,4]. Indentation of a thin elastic layer bonded to a rigid substrate has been analyzed in [5,6], while the study of the indentation by a rigid circular cylinder of finite length has been reported in [7,8]. The surface tension effects were considered in [9]- [12], the indentation of a functionally graded half-space in [13,14], and the micropolar elasticity effects in [15].

In this paper we derive the closed-form relationships between the depth of indentation and the applied force for cylindrical indentation of a half-space corresponding to different displacement datums and show that these relationships are nonlinear. The datum is first taken to be at the points x = ±b of the free-surface of a half-space.

The requirement that the work done by the indentation force must be equal to the work done by the contact pressure defines the minimum value of b for which the indentation is geometrically and physically possible. This minimum value is found to be about 10 times greater than the semi-width of the contact zone a = (4F R/πE * ) 1/2 .

The corresponding minimum value of the indentation depth is δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 , where δ 0 = 2F/πE * is the height of the contact zone. When the datum is taken to be at a point below the load, at some distance h from the free surface, there is an alternative closed-form expression for δ in terms of F , which involves the Poisson ratio ν. The minimum permissible value of h is h min ≈ 16.5a in the case ν = 0, and h min ≈ 27a in the case ν = 1/2, with the corresponding minimum indentation depth δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 . The relationship between h and b, corresponding to all indentation depths δ ≥ δ min , is shown to be linear for large values of h and b. The derived results are useful for the analysis of the indentation of large elastic blocks bonded to a rigid substrate either laterally or at its bottom.
2 Indentation by a rigid circular cylinder The equation of the circular contact in the (x, w) coordinate system for shallow indentation is

where w(0) = δ and w(±a) = δ -δ 0 (Fig. 1). By basic geometry, the contact semi-width a is the geometric mean of δ 0 and 2R -δ 0 , which for δ 0 ≪ R simplifies to a 2 = 2Rδ 0 , independently of b. A cylindrical indenter exerts a pressure p = p(x) over the surface of a half-space within the contact width 2a, which is statically equivalent to applied force F . In linear elasticity this contact pressure is semi-elliptical in shape and given by
div.formula: [1]-[4] R a z,w d x F d 0 initial free surface d R n) (E , 0 b
div.p: Figure 1: A rigid circular cylinder of radius R pressed into an elastic half-space by a force F (per unit length of the cylinder). The height of the cylindrical cap in contact with the material of a half-space is δ 0 , and 2a is the corresponding width of the contact. The depth of the indentation relative to the initial level of the free surface (x axis) is δ. The imposed displacement boundary condition is w(±b) = 0, where b ≥ b min and b min is determined in the body of the paper. The elastic constants of a half-space are E and ν.

The height of the contact zone δ 0 can then be obtained from the displacement expression of the Flamant's problem of a concentrated force on the boundary of a half-space [16] by using the superposition,
div.formula: δ 0 = 2 πE * a 0 p(x) ln a 2 x 2 -1 dx . (2.
div.p: The substitution of (2.2) into (2.3) and integration gives the following linear relationship between δ 0 and F , recognized that the actual indentation depth is different from the height of the contact zone (δ = δ 0 ) and that it depends on the boundary conditions.

The full elasticity solution of frictionless cylindrical indentation, which specifies the stress and displacement fields in the entire half-space, is available in the literature, where it is most commonly derived by using complex potentials, e.g., [17], p. 285. From this solution, it follows that the vertical displacement w = w(x) at any point on the surface of a half-space under semi-elliptical pressure distribution, subject to the displacement boundary condition w(±b) = 0, is
where
The plots of w(x) vs. For large values of b/a, (3.2) simplifies to
which demonstrates the logarithmic increase of δ/δ 0 with the increase of b/a.
The nonlinear relationship between δ and F can be readily recognized from (3.2) by observing that
div.formula: δ 0 = 1 2 Rf , a = Rf 1/2 , β = b R f -1/2 , f = F F * . (3.4) Substituting (3.4) into (3.2) gives δ = R 2    b R 2 - b R b R 2 -f + f ln   b R + b R 2 -f   - 1 2 f ln f    . (3

div.head: Minimum values b min and δ min
div.p: The material of the indented half-space, with a specified displacement boundary condition to eliminate the rigidbody translation, resists the penetration of the indenter. If the points of the free surface x = ±b are required to have zero vertical displacement throughout the indentation process, as the indentation force increases from 0 to its final value F , while the contact width increases from 0 to 2a = 2(F/F * ) 1/2 R, and the depth of indentation increases from 0 to its final value δ, there is a minimum value of b > a for which such indentation is geometrically and physically possible. For example, one cannot produce the penetration depth δ = δ 0 = 2F/(πE * ), if it is required that b = a, i.e., w(±a) = 0, and that the indenter and the free surface of a half-space are both initially at z = 0. This can be recognized from the fact that the work (per unit length of the cylinder) of the force F on the displacement δ 0 is not equal to the work of the pressure p(x) on the vertical displacement w a (x), i.e.,
div.formula: δ0 0 F ( δ)d δ = 1 2 a -a p(x)w a (x)dx , w a (x) = δ 0 1 - x 2 a 2 . (3.6)
div.p: Indeed, the integral on the left-hand side of (3.6) is equal to 2W 0 /3 and the integral on the right-hand side is equal to 3W 0 /4, where
. This means that one needs to find the minimum value of b > a, and the corresponding value of δ > δ 0 , such that
div.formula: W F = δ 0 F ( δ)d δ ≡ W p = 1 2 a -a p(x)w b (x)dx , w b (x) = δ -δ 0 x 2 a 2 .(3.7)
div.p: The indentation work condition W F = W p must hold because the indentation stress and strain fields in the halfspace are produced by the indentation force F , transmitted to the half-space by the statically equivalent pressure distribution p(x) = (2F/πa)(1 -x 2 /a 2 ) 1/2 . For example, for spherical indentation of a half-space F ∼ δ 3/2
and W F = W p = (2/5)F δ, while for conical indentation F ∼ δ 2 and W F = W p = (1/3)F δ.
The expressions for the works appearing in (3.7) can be conveniently determined from
div.formula: W F = δ 0 F ( δ)d δ = F δ - F 0 δd F ,(3.8)
W p = 1 2 a -a p(x)w b (x)dx = 1 2 F δ - 1 8 F δ 0 .(3.9)
div.p: Substituting expression (3.2) for δ, the pressure work W p in (3.9) becomes
In particular, if β = 1 this gives W p = 3W 0 /4. evaluated analytically. Toward that, it is convenient to rewrite the expression for δ from (3.2) as

div.head: The complementary work
div.p: and conveniently use â2 as the integration variable, such that d F = (πE * /4R) d(â 2 ). Upon a somewhat lengthy integration, it follows that
div.formula: W p = W F = W 0 1 4 + ln 2b a ,(3.13

div.head: Displacement along vertical axis below the indenter
div.p: The vertical displacement u z along the z axis beneath the indenter can be obtained from the complete solution of the cylindrical indentation problem by integrating the longitudinal strain ǫ z = ∂u z /∂z along the z axis, and by specifying the integration constant from the condition u z (z = 0) = δ. The longitudinal strain is, by Hooke's law,
where the stresses along the z axis are (e.g., [3], p. 92)
Thus, upon integration, the displacement along the z axis is found to be
div.formula: u z (z) = δ -δ 0 ln ζ + 1 + ζ 2 + ν 1 -ν ζ ζ -1 + ζ 2 . (4.
div.p: The plots of u z (z)/δ 0 vs. ζ = z/a are shown for three selected values of the indentation depth δ in Fig. 5. The displacement u z beneath the indenter is at first downwards (for sufficiently small z), but then it turns its direction upwards. For z ≫ a, (4.3) simplifies to

Expression (4.4) can be used in an approximate analysis of the indentation of a large elastic block bonded at its bottom to a rigid substrate. Suppose that, for a given indentation force F , one wants to specify the value of h, and thus δ, such that u z (z = h ≫ a) = 0. From (4.4), it then follows that If δ 0 in (4.5) is replaced with 2F/(πE * ), as given by (2.4), expression (4.5) coincides with expression (5.58), p.
131, of [2], which was used in [4], p. 101, to discuss the indentation of a large elastic block bonded at its bottom to a rigid substrate.

The relationship between the height h and the horizontal distance b used to define the displacement datum x = ±b on the free surface in section 3 can also be established. When (4.5) is equated to (3.3), if follows 5 Nonlinear δ = δ(F, h) relationship
The nonlinear relationship δ = δ(F, h) can be derived from (4.3). If the displacement is required to be zero at the point z = h below the indenter, from (4.3) it is found that
div.formula: δ = δ 0 ln η + 1 + η 2 + ν 1 -ν η η -1 + η 2 , η = h a .(5.1)
div.p: Because δ 0 = (F/F * )R/2 and a = (F/F * ) 1/2 R, expression (5.1) can be rewritten as
div.formula: δ R = 1 2 f ln η/ f + 1 + η 2 /f + ν 1 -ν (η/ f ) η/ f -1 + η 2 /f , f = F F * .(5.2)
div.p: This is an explicit form of the nonlinear relationship δ = δ(F, h). The plot of δ/δ 0 vs. h/a is shown in Fig. 6a.
div.formula: For η 2 /f = (h/R) 2 (F * /F ) ≫ 1, (5.2) simplifies to δ R = 1 4 F F * ln F * F + 2 ln 2h R - ν 1 -ν .(5.3)
div.p: The relationship (5.2) for δ = δ(F, h) involves the Poisson ratio ν, while the relationship (3.5) for δ = δ(F, b)
does not (apart from the dependence of F * on ν), which is as expected because the relationship between h and b, such as (4.7), involves the Poisson ratio.

div.head: Minimum values h min and δ min
div.p: Similarly to the analysis of b min and the corresponding δ min from section 3, one can determine h min and the corresponding δ min by requiring that W F = W p (to within a prescribed numerical accuracy). By the same analysis as in section 3, it follows that the pressure work is
while the work of the force F is

The plot of the work ratio W F /W p with η = h/a is shown in Fig. 6b for three selected values of the Poisson ratio. For η ≫ 1, the work expressions (5.4) and (5.5) both simplify to which explains the horizontal plateau W F /W p = 1 in Fig. 6b, rapidly approached with the increase of h/a. For h = a, the two works are clearly different,

For example, for ν = 1/3, (5.7) gives W p = 0.4243W 0 and W F = 0.4908W 0 , their ratio being W F /W p = 1.157. This means that one cannot produce indentation with the contact width 2a if it is required that u z (z = a) = 0 and that the indenter and the free surface of a half-space are both initially at z = 0. As a matter of fact, if h = a, the indentation depth predicted by (5.1) would be less than δ 0 , i.e., δ ≈ 0.674δ 0 , meaning that in section 3 b < a, which does not correspond to physical indentation from the z = 0 initial surface level.
The minimum indentation depth δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 = 3.5(1 -ν 2 )(2F/πE) obtained in section 3 when b =

As discussed earlier, the h-datum is of practical interest for an approximate analysis of the indentation of a thick block constrained at its bottom by being bonded to a rigid substrate (Fig. 7a). In this case the values of b corresponding to h ≫ a, define the portion of the free surface |x| < b which is displaced downwards relative to the initial un-indented free surface z = 0, while the points |x| > b are displaced upwards. Furthermore, the b-datum itself, with a large value of b ≫ a, may be of interest for an approximate analysis of the indentation of a wide block of material supported laterally, rather than at its bottom, in which case the constraints at x = ±b prevent vertical displacement of these points (Fig. 7b). In both cases shown in Fig. 7 the stress and strain fields in the contact region are nearly equal to that in a half-space, and the dominant term in the expression for the indentation depth δ/δ 0 is still the logarithmic term ln(2b/a) or ln(2h/a); see (3.3) and (4.5). This can be verified by using the finite element method to satisfy the remote displacement and traction boundary conditions at z = h and x = ±b, which is beyond the scope of the present paper.

The general relationship between b and h, corresponding to the same indentation depth δ, is obtained by equating expressions (3.2) and (5.1), ). Figure 8b shows the variation of δ/δ 0 vs. b/a and h/a in the case ν = 1/3, as determined from (3.2) and (5.1). The actual indentation depths are above the minimum indentation depth δ min = 3.5δ 0 , shown by a dash-dotted horizontal line.

div.head: Conclusion
div.p: A closed-form nonlinear relationship δ = δ(F, b) between the depth of indentation δ and the applied force F for cylindrical indentation of an elastic half-space, with the displacement datum at the points x = ±b of the free surface, is derived and is given by (3.2). The requirement that the work done by the indentation force must be equal to the work done by the contact pressure (W F = W p ) specifies the minimum value of b for which the indentation is geometrically and physically possible, and thus the minimum value of the indentation depth δ. The value of b min is found to be about 10 times greater than the semi-width of the contact zone a = (4RF/πE * ) 1/2 , where R is the radius of the cylindrical indenter and E * = E/(1 -ν 2 ) is the effective modulus of elasticity of the indented half-space. The corresponding minimum indentation depth is δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 , where δ 0 = 2F/πE * is the height of the contact zone, which is independent of b. When the displacement datum is taken to be at a point along the axis of symmetry below the load, at some distance h from the free surface, there is a closed-form expression for δ = δ(F, h), which is dependent on the Poisson ratio and is given by (5.2). The work requirement W F = W p specifies again the minimum value of h for which the indentation is geometrically and physically possible. This value is found to be h min ≈ 16.5a in the case ν = 0, about 21a in the case ν = 1/3, and about 27a in the case ν = 1/2, with the corresponding minimum indentation depth δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 . The relationship between h and b, corresponding to the same indentation depth δ ≥ δ min , is shown to be linear for large values of h and b. The obtained results may be useful in the analysis of the indentation of elastic blocks of finite size bonded to a rigid substrate laterally or at its bottom.

figure.head: Figure 1
figure.figDesc: Figure1shows the cross section of a smooth rigid cylindrical indenter of radius R pressed against an isotropic elastic half-space whose elastic constants are E and ν. The depth of the indentation due to the vertical force F (per unit length of an infinitely long cylinder), relative to the initial level of the free surface (x axis), is denoted by δ. The value of δ corresponds to a selected value of b ≥ b min , which defines the points x = ±b of the free surface at which the vertical displacement is required to be zero, w(x = ±b) = 0. The height of the cylindrical cap in contact with the material of a half-space is δ 0 (indenter penetration depth into the material), and 2a is the corresponding width of the contact. The objective is to determine b min and δ min , and the relationship δ = δ(F, b)

figure.head: . 4 ) 1 Figure 2 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 2: The normalized displacement w(x)/δ 0 vs. x/a for two values of b. The initial position of the free surface of the half-space is shown by a dash-dotted line. The two shown curves differ by vertical translation of amount 0.4δ 0 , where δ 0 = 2F/πE * .

x/a from (3.1) are shown in Fig. 2. Two values of β = b/a are used, corresponding to b = 10a and b = 15a, where a = (F/F * ) 1/2 R and F * = (π/4)E * R. The displacement w(x) is normalized with δ 0 = (R/2)F/F * . The value w(0) in the case b = 10a is δ = 3.5δ 0 , while δ = 3.9δ 0 for b = 15a. The two displacements differ by the vertical translation δ b=15a -δ b=10a = 0.4δ 0 .

This is an explicit form of the nonlinear force-indentation relationship δ = δ(F, b), which is plotted in Fig.3for several values of the ratio b/R. The nonlinearity of δ = δ(F, b) relationship is more pronounced for smaller values of b. The maximum applied force for the indentation to remain elastic is F max = 10 -n F * , where for metals typically n ≥ 3.

figure.head: Figure 3 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 3: The nonlinear variation of the normalized indentation depth 10 3 (δ/R) with the normalized indentation force 10 3 (F/F * ), where F * = πE * R/4. The nonlinearity is particularly pronounced for the smaller values of the ratio b/R.

figure.head: F0Figure 4 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 4: (a) The decrease of the work ratio W p /W F toward the value of 1 with the increase of b/a. For b > b min ≈ 10a, the work ratio falls below the value of 1.00013. (b) The variation of δ/δ 0 with b/a. The values of δ for b ≥ b min ≈ 10a correspond to actual indentation.

If β = 1, this gives W F = 2W 0 /3, which is different from W p = 3W 0 /4, their ratio being W p /W F = 9/8 = 1.125.The plot of the work ratio W p /W F versus b/a is shown in Fig.4a. For β = 10, (3.10) and (3.12) give W p = 3.244479W 0 and W F = 3.244061W 0 , which can be considered to be approximately equal to each other, the relative error being (W p -W F )/W p = 1.3 × 10 -4 W 0 . We therefore define b min ≈ 10a, the corresponding (minimum) indentation depth being δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 . For β ≫ 1, (3.10) and (3.12) both become

) which corresponds to the horizontal plateau W p /W F = 1 in Fig. 4a, rapidly approached as b/a is increased. The variation of δ with b/a is shown in Fig. 4b for the values of b ≥ a, keeping in mind that the actual indentations correspond to b ≥ b min . For large values of β = b/a, the approximation applies δ = δ 0 (k + ln β) , k = 1 2 + ln 2 ≈ 1.1931 . (3.14) For example, the indentation depth is δ ≈ 8.1δ 0 for b = 10 3 a, while δ ≈ 10.4δ 0 for b = 10 4 a. From (3.14), one can also express explicitly the value of b = a exp(δ/δ 0 -k) corresponding to any given indentation depth δ ≥ δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 . Although all values of b ≥ b min are conceptually important, large values of b, much greater than b min , are most useful for the analysis of indentation of large, but finite size blocks of material.

figure.head: Figure 5 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 5: The variation of the normalized displacement u z (z)/δ 0 with ζ = z/a for ν = 1/3 and for three shown values of the indentation depth δ, where δ 0 = 2F/πE * and a = (4RF/πE * ) 1/2 . The selected values of δ correspond to the h-datums of 21a, 95a and 427a, as discussed in the text.

This gives the linear relationship between b and h, b= h exp -1 2(1 -ν) ,(4.7)with the coefficient dependent on the Poisson ratio only. For example, if h = 10 3 a then b = 472.4a, with the corresponding depth of indentation δ = 7.35δ 0 ; if h = 10 4 a then b = 4724a and δ = 9.65δ 0 . In both cases, the Poisson ratio was taken to be ν = 1/3. Although derived for large values of b and h, the linear relationship (4.7) approximately holds even for the values of b and h near b min and h min , as recognized by numerical evaluations (see Fig. 8 below).

figure.head: Figure 6 :Figure 7 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 6: (a) The variation of δ/δ 0 with h/a. The results are relevant for h ≥ h min , dependent on the Poisson ratio, as discussed in the body of the paper. For large values of h/a, the normalized indentation depth is approximately δ/δ 0 = ln(2h/a) -ν/[2(1 -ν)]. (b) The decrease of the work ratio W F /W p toward the value of 1 with the increase of h/a. Note that the normalizing factors δ 0 and a are also dependent on the Poisson ratio, because δ 0 = (1 -ν 2 )(2F/πE) and a = [4(1 -ν 2 )F R/πE] 1/2 .

figure.head: Figure 8 :
figure.figDesc: Figure 8: (a) The relationship between h/a and b/a, corresponding to the same indentation δ, as obtained from (5.7). For large values of b/a, the linear relationship (4.7) holds, with the slope h/b = exp[0.5/(1 -ν)]. (b) The variation of δ/δ 0 vs. b/a and h/a in the case ν = 1/3. The actual indentation depths are above the minimum indentation depth δ min ≈ 3.5δ 0 , shown by the dotted horizontal line. b min ≈ 10δ 0 = 10(1 -ν 2 )(2F/πE), corresponds to h min = 16.45a in the case ν = 0, h min = 19.44a in the case ν = 1/4, h min = 21.13a in the case ν = 1/3, and h min = 27.13a in the case ν = 1/2, where a = [4(1 -ν 2 )F R/πE] 1/2 .

If this is numerically solved for η = h/a in terms of β = b/a, the plots shown in Fig. 8a are obtained. The relationship between h and b is almost linear in the entire range of b/a > 50 or so, particularly for greater values of ν. For large values of b/a, it is well approximated by h = b exp[0.5/(1 -ν)], following from (4.7

monogr.title: Contact Problems in the Classical Theory of Elasticity

analytic.title: The contact problem of a rigid cylinder on an elastic layer
Indentation of an elastic layer by a rigid cylinder

monogr.title: On the compression of a cylinder in contact with a plane surface

analytic.title: Normal line contact of finite-length cylinders
Analysis of two-dimensional contact problems considering surface effect
Analytic relations for two-dimensional indentations with surface tension
Cylindrical indentation of an elastic bonded layer with surface tension
Surface effects on cylindrical indentation of a soft layer on a rigid substrate
Plane strain cylindrical indentation of functionally graded half-plane with exponentially varying shear modulus in the presence of residual surface tension
Two-dimensional contact analysis of elastic graded materials
